model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"In fixed-step mode, the current MLP only sees a small fraction of the training set. Raising batch size to 256 should expose far more examples within the same 256 updates while keeping the rest of the now-improved optimization setup unchanged.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"            if USE_BATCHNORM:\n                layers.append(nn.LayerNorm(hidden_dim))","old":"            if USE_BATCHNORM:\n                layers.append(nn.BatchNorm1d(hidden_dim))","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"With only about 1 to 2 effective passes through the data in fixed-step mode, BatchNorm running statistics can be a weak fit at evaluation time. Switching the hidden-layer normalization to LayerNorm keeps the stabilization benefit without depending on batch-level state.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"            if USE_BATCHNORM:\n                layers.append(nn.BatchNorm1d(hidden_dim))","old":"            if USE_BATCHNORM:\n                layers.append(nn.LayerNorm(hidden_dim))","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest verified gain came from increasing batch size under the fixed 256-step budget. This pushes that idea further to expose even more training examples per run, while reverting the normalization change that degraded loss.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest improvements have come from exposing more training examples within the fixed 256-update budget. Increasing batch size again to 1024 should push the MLP through several effective epochs while leaving the optimizer and scheduler unchanged.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The larger fixed-step batches have continued to improve loss, which usually leaves room for a higher AdamW learning rate. Raising the peak rate should make better use of the now much lower-gradient-noise regime without changing the overall schedule shape.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"After improving the fixed-step optimization path, the remaining bottleneck is likely model capacity. Increasing the base hidden width from 192 to 256 keeps the architecture simple while giving the flat MLP a materially stronger classifier under the same training recipe.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current run is improving steadily with more data exposure and a slightly wider MLP, so the next simple capacity increase is one additional hidden layer. A third GELU block should give the flat model more representational power without changing the successful optimizer and batching setup.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"CHANNEL_MULT = 3","old":"CHANNEL_MULT = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The deeper, wider MLP is still improving, so the next clean capacity increase is to widen the later hidden blocks without changing the first projection too aggressively. Increasing the channel multiplier should strengthen the classifier head while preserving the successful training recipe.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-3","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The model has become substantially larger and is now seeing several effective epochs within the fixed update budget, so the current AdamW decay may be over-regularizing. Reducing weight decay is a simple way to let the expanded MLP fit more of the signal without changing the successful optimizer family or schedule.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-3","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The reduced weight decay consistently landed just behind the best visible result, so the next safe move is to restore the stronger AdamW regularization that paired best with the larger, deeper MLP. That re-establishes the best-known configuration before spending the remaining budget on new directions.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The current best configuration appears stable, and the earlier learning-rate increase still produced a small verified gain. A modest additional step up in AdamW peak learning rate is a low-complexity way to test whether the larger, better-regularized MLP can benefit from slightly more aggressive optimization.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.1","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The higher learning rate regressed slightly, so this returns to the better peak rate and tests a small amount of dropout on the now larger, multi-epoch MLP. That is a simple way to probe whether mild activation regularization improves validation loss once the model has enough capacity to benefit from it.","secondary_modes":["topk","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.1","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1536","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Dropout clearly hurt this regime, so this removes it and uses the remaining budget to push the fixed-step exposure idea a bit further. A moderate batch increase to 1536 tests whether more examples per update still help without jumping all the way to a much more expensive doubling.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 2048","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 1536","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest gains are still coming from increasing how much of CIFAR-10 the model sees within the fixed 256-step budget. Pushing batch size to 2048 is the next direct test of that trend while keeping the rest of the best-known recipe unchanged.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 3072","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 2048","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The fixed-step exposure scaling continues to be the dominant source of verified gains. Increasing batch size again, but by a smaller factor than a full doubling, tests whether that trend still holds while keeping the compute jump more controlled.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"At batch size 3072 the optimization regime is much lower-noise than before, so the best-known 2.5e-3 learning rate may now be conservative. A modest increase is a cheap way to test whether the larger effective batch can support faster progress without another large runtime jump.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 4096","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 3072","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last learning-rate increase regressed, while larger fixed-step batches have been the clearest source of improvement. For the final attempt, restore the better peak rate and push the exposure-driven strategy one more step by increasing batch size again.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}
